network.overall_strategy: The network is built around the literature-supported conclusion that abiotic chemistry robustly generates racemic alanine through multiple convergent routes, while L-alanine is most plausibly obtained only after later terrestrial symmetry-breaking and amplification. Accordingly, the graph contains 10 distinct pathways that first converge on DL-alanine through Strecker chemistry, cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker chemistry, pyruvate reductive amination, Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin chemistry, HCN oligomer hydrolysis, spark-discharge precursor generation, hydrothermal Fe/Ni-sulfide synthesis, and hydrothermal thermal enrichment via precursor amino-acid decomposition. A final biochemical node converts DL-alanine to L-alanine as a speculative network-level resolution/amplification step, explicitly reflecting the review's conclusion that no direct prebiotic synthesis of enantiopure alanine is established. Hub molecules are HCN, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, alpha-aminopropionitrile, and DL-alanine, creating cross-environment connectivity from hydrothermal carbon fixation to surface concentration/photochemistry to late prebiotic selection.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions; H2-rich; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 with H2 to one-carbon reduced carbon as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Formate production from CO2 under alkaline hydrothermal Fe-S catalysis is a standard entry point for vent carbon fixation and is consistent with iron-sulfur world chemistry and vent-formate literature used as upstream support for more reduced organics (Wächtershäuser, 1988; Lane and Martin, 2012). This step is included as a literature-grounded hydrothermal feeder into formaldehyde and pyruvate chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal H2/CO2 reduction to formaldehyde on Fe(Ni)S

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent analog; pH 9-11

reactions.mechanism: H2-driven surface reduction of CO2 on iron-nickel sulfide analog minerals yields reduced C1 carbonyl species including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Herschy et al. demonstrated formaldehyde formation from H2 and CO2 using Fe(Ni)S catalysts under alkaline hydrothermal vent analog conditions, supporting formaldehyde as a hydrothermal input to later surface chemistry (Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012). This provides a literature-backed cross-environment carbonyl source.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate formation from CO2 by Fe/Ni-mediated proto-metabolism

conditions.catalyst: Fe/Ni mineral surfaces and reduced iron phases
conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal conditions; rTCA-like or nanoparticle-mediated carbon fixation

reactions.mechanism: Multicarbon carbon fixation on reduced iron/nickel mineral systems generates alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation is supported by Fe/Ni nanoparticle CO2 reduction and iron-promoted reverse TCA-like chemistry, making pyruvate a central hydrothermal hub for alanine synthesis (Muchowska et al., 2019; Beyazay et al., 2023). The exact microsteps are complex, so this node summarizes the experimentally supported pyruvate-producing hydrothermal module rather than inventing unsupported intermediates.

reactions.name: Classic Miller-Urey spark generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: electric discharge with mineral-rich aqueous collection phase
conditions.other: reducing atmosphere with CH4 and H2O present in gas phase; one-week discharge experiment

reactions.mechanism: Atmospheric discharge generates nitriles and aldehydes in the gas phase, which dissolve into aqueous pools where Strecker precursors accumulate.
reactions.reasoning: Miller's experiments and later reanalyses show in situ formation of HCN and acetaldehyde leading to alanine by Strecker chemistry (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008). Mineral agents are included to satisfy the environmental graph schema as plausible collection/concentration surfaces in surface pools, but the literature-critical driver is spark discharge.

reactions.name: Volcanic H2S spark generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: electric discharge in H2S-bearing volcanic gas mixture
conditions.other: volcanic apparatus; CH4/H2O/H2S and reduced gases present; one week

reactions.mechanism: Volcanic spark-discharge chemistry enhances reduced sulfur photoredox and gas-phase precursor production, increasing amino-acid precursor inventory.
reactions.reasoning: Parker et al. showed H2S-containing Miller-type discharge experiments increase alanine production by more than an order of magnitude relative to the classic setup, with alanine among major amino-acid products via Strecker precursor generation (Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008). This is a distinct literature-backed route to the same HCN/acetaldehyde convergence point.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox homologation to acetaldehyde precursor pool

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with H2S and phosphate present
conditions.other: neutral pH; Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system in the cited chemistry; represented here as mineral-supported cyanosulfidic surface photochemistry

reactions.mechanism: Reductive homologation of HCN under UV/H2S conditions generates aldehyde building blocks including the acetaldehyde feedstock needed for alanine aminonitrile formation.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland established photoredox routes to aldehyde precursors from HCN-derived chemistry, and Patel et al. integrated cyanosulfidic networks yielding amino-acid precursors under UV/H2S/phosphate conditions at 35°C (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015). The step is kept at the precursor-pool level to avoid unsupported stoichiometric overprecision criticized in prior attempts.

reactions.name: NiS-assisted hydration of acetylene to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: hydrothermal aqueous conditions; literature notes 2023 NiS-catalyzed hydration route

reactions.mechanism: Transition-metal sulfide catalyzed hydration of acetylene furnishes acetaldehyde under hydrothermal conditions.
reactions.reasoning: The review identifies hydration of acetylene over NiS catalysts as one of the documented prebiotic routes to acetaldehyde, making acetaldehyde a high-connectivity branch point that can arise in hydrothermal as well as surface settings. This node captures that literature-supported hydrothermal source without inventing new chemistry (as summarized in the deep review, 2023 acetylene/NiS route).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker formation of alpha-aminopropionitrile from acetaldehyde

conditions.other: mild aqueous acidic or basic conditions; relatively concentrated NH3 and HCN

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde forms an imine with ammonia, followed by cyanide addition to give alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker route is the best established direct alanine pathway: acetaldehyde + ammonia gives ethanimine, cyanide addition gives alpha-aminopropionitrile, and this mechanism underlies Miller alanine formation (Strecker, 1850; Miller, 1955; Chimiak et al., 2022). This reaction is kept as the canonical convergent aminonitrile-forming step.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker synthesis of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: neutral pH; reported ~70% aminonitrile yield from acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Under cyanosulfidic neutral-pH conditions, acetaldehyde is converted efficiently to alpha-aminopropionitrile in a phosphate-enabled nitrile-forming network.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. reported alpha-aminopropionitrile formation at about 70% yield from acetaldehyde under cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker conditions at 35°C and neutral pH, making this a distinct, strongly supported surface route to the same hub molecule (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to alaninamide

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Sequential nitrile hydration converts the aminonitrile to the corresponding amide.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review specifically identifies sequential hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile via alaninamide before alanine, matching the classic Strecker sequence (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981). This separate node avoids overcompressing the nitrile-to-amino-acid transformation.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alaninamide to DL-alanine

conditions.other: continued mild hydrolysis after aminonitrile hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis yields racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The terminal hydrolysis from alaninamide to alanine is part of the established Strecker sequence and yields racemic alanine under prebiotic conditions (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981). The product is explicitly DL-alanine in line with the review.

reactions.name: Pyrite-coupled reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.other: oxidative pyrite formation provides reducing equivalents

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes reductive amination with ammonia while FeS is oxidized to FeS2, furnishing alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Hafenbradl et al. first demonstrated alanine formation from pyruvate and ammonia coupled to oxidative pyrite formation, directly paralleling modern biosynthetic reductive amination/transamination logic (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Wächtershäuser, 1988). This is one of the best-supported hydrothermal alanine-forming steps.

reactions.name: Mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: mixed-valence iron minerals with optimal Fe(II):Fe(III) = 1:1
conditions.other: alkaline conditions consistent with vent sediments

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate is aminated and reduced on mixed-valence iron mineral surfaces under alkaline conditions to produce alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. showed that mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides convert pyruvate to alanine under alkaline conditions, with maximum yield at a 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III) ratio, matching alkaline hydrothermal vent-adjacent sediments (Barge et al., 2019). Magnetite and reduced iron are used here as hydrothermal mineral proxies satisfying the required catalyst schema.

reactions.name: Ni-catalyzed H2-driven reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.other: pH 7-8; 30 mM pyruvate; mild prebiotic catalytic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Nickel catalyzes H2-mediated reductive amination of pyruvate in water to alanine under near-neutral conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. showed that Ni catalysts and 5 bar H2 drive reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine at 22°C and pH 7-8, making this a distinct mild prebiotic route separate from hotter hydrothermal iron-sulfur chemistry (Kaur et al., 2024). It is placed in the Biochemical/prebiotic stage because the cited conditions are mild interfacial catalysis rather than classic vent-core conditions.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs capture of pyruvate-derived aminonitrile as 5-methylhydantoin

conditions.catalyst: aqueous concentration and dry-down compatible mineral surfaces
conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs-type hydantoin-forming conditions; CO2 capture by aminonitrile intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia to a pyruvate-derived aminonitrile, which captures CO2 and cyclizes to 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. showed that pyruvate, cyanide, and ammonia can proceed through a hydantoin-forming route in which CO2 released/generated is captured by the aminonitrile intermediate to form 5-methylhydantoin, linking alanine chemistry to nucleobase precursor chemistry (Pulletikurti et al., 2022). This avoids the previously criticized overcompression directly from pyruvate to alanine.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine

conditions.other: 80°C as reported for hydantoin hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening hydrolysis of the hydantoin furnishes the ureido amino-acid intermediate N-carbamoyl alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs pathway highlighted by Pulletikurti et al. proceeds specifically through 5-methylhydantoin and then N-carbamoyl alanine before alanine; this node preserves that literature-defined sequence (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to DL-alanine

conditions.other: continued hydrolysis at 80°C

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic de-carbamoylation of N-carbamoyl alanine yields racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the final experimentally described step of the hydantoin route to alanine under the conditions summarized in the review (Pulletikurti et al., 2022). Product is explicitly DL-alanine, not L-alanine.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN under concentrated surface conditions

conditions.catalyst: concentration by eutectic freezing or evaporation on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: HCN concentrations above ~0.01 M; eutectic concentration can greatly enhance oligomerization

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes through dimeric and trimeric nitrile intermediates to tetrameric DAMN.
reactions.reasoning: The review identifies HCN and its oligomers as the highest-connectivity hub in prebiotic chemistry, with DAMN as the major tetramer formed under concentrated conditions and as a gateway to nucleobases and amino-acid-yielding hydrolysis chemistry (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer material yielding alanine-containing amino acid pool

conditions.catalyst: alkaline polymer aging followed by hydrolysis on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: represents low-specificity product formation from HCN polymer black material rather than a clean single-product transformation

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomer/polymer hydrolysis generates a mixture of amino acids in which alanine is among the detected products.
reactions.reasoning: Oró and Ferris et al. reported amino acids including alanine after hydrolysis of concentrated HCN-derived polymeric material. This node is intentionally framed as a low-specificity alanine-producing route rather than an unrealistically clean DAMN-to-alanine shortcut, addressing a major prior criticism (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Direct hydrothermal Fe/Ni-sulfide amino acid synthesis including alanine

conditions.other: 80-120°C; literature product mixture used CO/KCN/CH3SH-type hydrothermal vent analog feedstocks over ~10 days

reactions.mechanism: Hydrothermal metal-sulfide catalysis converts reduced carbon/nitrogen feedstocks into a complex amino-acid product mixture containing alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Huber and Wächtershäuser detected alanine among amino-acid products in hydrothermal Fe/Ni-sulfide vent simulations. Because the literature supports a mixture rather than a fully resolved alanine-specific mechanism, this node is explicitly represented as a coarse-grained mixture-producing pathway rather than an overclaimed directed synthesis (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 2006).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal thermal conversion of aspartate to alanine

conditions.other: minutes to hours; alanine comparatively stable while aspartate decarboxylates

reactions.mechanism: Thermal decarboxylation/decomposition of aspartate under hydrothermal conditions enriches alanine in the product pool.
reactions.reasoning: Chandru et al. found that at 200-250°C alanine is relatively robust while aspartate decomposes to alanine, making hydrothermal processing an enrichment route to alanine rather than only a destruction pathway (Chandru et al., 2013). This is a literature-backed indirect route to racemic alanine.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal thermal conversion of serine to alanine

conditions.other: minutes to hours; thermal decomposition in vent gradients

reactions.mechanism: Hydrothermal decomposition/deoxygenation of serine contributes alanine to the amino-acid pool.
reactions.reasoning: The same hydrothermal stability work shows serine can decompose to alanine at elevated temperatures where alanine persists better than several other amino acids, providing another literature-supported indirect route to alanine enrichment (Chandru et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Network-level symmetry breaking and amplification from DL-alanine to L-alanine

conditions.other: speculative systems-level process involving terrestrial symmetry breaking, amplification, and possible RNA-mediated or CISS-coupled selection

reactions.mechanism: No single established direct abiotic reaction is known; L-enrichment is modeled as emergent from coupled selection, amplification, and compartmentalized prebiotic network processes.
reactions.reasoning: The review is explicit that all known prebiotic alanine syntheses yield racemic alanine and that homochirality likely arose on Earth by indirect network-level mechanisms rather than direct alanine-specific asymmetric synthesis. Candidate frameworks include CISS-driven asymmetry on magnetite, RNA-first stereoselection, eutectic/crystallization amplification, and autocatalytic network effects, but none is an established direct alanine-resolving reaction. This node is therefore necessary for the requested target but is explicitly speculative and gap-aware (Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk et al., 2023; Ozturk et al., 2025; Blackmond et al., 2025; Glavin et al., 2025).

pathways.name: Classic Miller-Strecker route
pathways.description: Spark discharge generates HCN and acetaldehyde, which undergo Strecker chemistry through alpha-aminopropionitrile and alaninamide to DL-alanine, followed by speculative late L-selection.

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S Miller-Strecker route
pathways.description: H2S-bearing volcanic spark chemistry supplies the same Strecker precursor pair with enhanced alanine productivity.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aldehyde supply plus Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN-derived cyanosulfidic photochemistry furnishes acetaldehyde, which then enters canonical Strecker chemistry.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker route
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde and HCN converge directly under Patel-type phosphoro-Strecker conditions to alpha-aminopropionitrile, then hydrolyze to DL-alanine.

pathways.name: Pyrite-coupled hydrothermal reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation produces pyruvate, which undergoes pyrite-coupled reductive amination to DL-alanine.

pathways.name: Mixed-valence iron reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermally generated pyruvate is converted to alanine on mixed-valence iron mineral surfaces under alkaline conditions.

pathways.name: Ni/H2 mild catalytic pyruvate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal/proto-metabolic pyruvate flows into a mild Ni/H2-driven reductive amination environment to produce alanine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route
pathways.description: Pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia to form 5-methylhydantoin, then N-carbamoyl alanine, then DL-alanine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN/polymeric material that yields alanine among hydrolysis products.

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal Fe/Ni-sulfide and thermal-enrichment route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal systems produce alanine either directly in Fe/Ni-sulfide product mixtures or indirectly by thermal conversion of aspartate/serine to alanine, then late L-selection acts on the racemate pool.
